title: Financial constraints, cash flow timing patterns, and asset prices
abstract: We show that firms collect almost 70% of their cash flows in the second half of the fiscal year, and that firms that collect more cash by year-end earn a 6.8% higher per annum risk premium and save more cash. We rationalize these facts in a quantitative investment-based asset pricing model. Immediate cash payments negatively affect profitability, but reduce equity financing costs by increasing information transparency. Financially constrained firms optimally collect more cash at year-end when firms’ performance attracts more attention and information transparency is more valuable. Such behavior further results in greater exposure to aggregate productivity and financial shocks.

author.Weiping Hu: Peking University HSBC Business School, Nanshan District, Shenzhen, 518055, China
author.Kai Li*: Peking University HSBC Business School and PHBS Sargent Institute of Quantitative Economics and Finance, Nanshan District, Shenzhen, 518055, China
author.Xiao Zhang: Chinese Academy of Finance and Development, Central University of Finance and Economics, Haidian District, Beijing, 100081, China

article.1. Introduction: Using US firm-level data, we show that cash flows display strong timing patterns: specifically, firms receive almost 70% of their cash flows in the second half of the year.1 Most empirical and theoretical studies of cash flow volatility have focused on exogenous cash flow shocks and their impact on firm decisions.2 This paper, however, studies firms’ endogenous decision with respect to cash flow timing patterns with financial constraints, and the implications of this decision for corporate policies and the cross-section of stock returns. The central empirical finding is that firms that collect more cash by year-end earn higher expected stock returns and save more cash. To comprehensively explain these empirical observations, we develop a dynamic investment-based model, which offers mechanisms that underlie the relationships between cash flow timing patterns, corporate investment and cash-saving decisions, and their impact on financial outcomes.Our model establishes a connection between year-end cash collection and financing costs, consistent with the empirical observations. The key intuition is that, when selling goods to customers, firms can receive cash or unpaid revenue that is composed of working capital, such as account receivables and inventory investment. Requiring cash payments is costly because a supplier must offer customers a substantial discount as a reward for paying cash. Meanwhile, unpaid revenue is an important indicator of information transparency.3 A higher unpaid revenue indicates lower transparency, resulting in higher information asymmetry and financing costs (Francis et al., 2004, García-Teruel et al., 2014). Since year-end performance attracts more attention, firms have an incentive to collect cash by year-end and lower their financing costs by decreasing information asymmetry. This is particularly relevant for financially constrained firms, which have a greater incentive to collect cash flows by year-end to improve information transparency. In summary, cash flow timing patterns provide valuable insights into firm fundamentals and firms’ exposure to aggregate risks, including implications for asset prices and real quantities.We begin by presenting four empirical facts about cash flow timing patterns. First, the patterns are prevalent at an aggregate level and persist over decades. Second, financially constrained firms show a more significant cash flow timing pattern, suggesting that year-end cash collection (hereafter, YCC)4 is positively related to financial constraints. Third, financially constrained firms with higher year-end cash collection earn a significantly higher risk premium, which we refer to as the year-end cash collection premium. Finally, we show that the year-end cash collection is mainly driven by the within-year spread of inventories and receivables, and that the YCC premium is not simply driven by annual inventory growth (Belo and Lin, 2012) and trade credit effects (Grigoris et al., 2023).Motivated by these empirical facts, we propose a dynamic investment-based model to explain the positive relationship between financial constraints, operating cash flow spread (hereafter, OCF spread),5 and the YCC premium. In this model, firms produce output using a stochastic technology that is subject to both idiosyncratic and aggregate productivity shocks. Aggregate issuance cost shocks are stochastic and independent of aggregate productivity shocks. Firms make investment, cash savings, cash payments, equity financing, and payout decisions to maximize their value. Idiosyncratic productivity shocks drive cross-sectional heterogeneity. Our model has three key features. First, sales are not immediately paid in cash. Demand for immediate cash payments is costly, as suppliers must offer a substantial discount as a reward for paying cash; thus, higher demand for cash payments decreases firms’ profitability. Second, equity issuance costs are a function of cash collection. A higher collection of cash payments results in lower information asymmetry and lower equity issuance costs. Third, we assume a deterministic regime shift in external attention. The literature shows that fiscal year-end earnings reports attract more attention (Brown and Pinello, 2007, Fan et al., 2010, Frankel et al., 2017). Financially constrained firms have high financing needs and are more likely to collect cash late in the year to increase the transparency of earnings information, which can lower incurred external financing costs.The cross-sectional variation in expected stock returns arises endogenously in our model because of different degrees of exposure to aggregate productivity and financial shocks. Financially constrained firms with high equity financing needs collect cash payments by year-end to avoid the high agency cost of year-end unpaid revenue. Therefore, a positive relationship between YCC and financial constraints is well established. We then document that a high YCC firm is riskier for two reasons. First, high YCC firms demand more cash payments for current sales. The future cash flows of high YCC firms primarily consist of immediate payments, and thus high YCC firms are more positively exposed to aggregate productivity shocks. Second, firms that collect more cash flows by year-end decrease their operating cash flows in the next period, meaning that they are more likely to issue equity in the future. Thus, high YCC firms are more positively exposed to aggregate financial shocks. Our findings show that year-end cash collection leads to more risk exposure and higher stock returns.Our model also suggests the presence of cross-sectional variation in cash decisions. First, we find that higher YCC firms, which have a more positive relationship between expected marginal financing costs and the discount factor, have a greater incentive to save cash. Specifically, high YCC firms have high financing needs in the next period so that they are more influenced by aggregate financial shocks. In the spirit of Palazzo (2012), the positive relation between expected marginal financing costs and the discount factor (i.e., higher expected marginal financing costs associated with higher marginal utility) results in a higher marginal value of cash. Additionally, we also find an apparent pattern of cash holdings within a year in high YCC firms; that is, firms tend to hold more cash in the second half of the year relative to the first half. This seasonal change in cash holdings is closely related to year-end cash collection because there is a substitutable relationship between unpaid revenue and cash holdings (Opler et al., 1999). Specifically, firms would substitute cash holdings for unpaid revenue at year-end when unpaid revenue is disciplined the most. Therefore, at year-end, high YCC firms without high unpaid revenue carried over to the next period have a greater incentive to hold cash than they do in the first half of the year.Quantitatively, the baseline model with reasonable parameter values closely matches the aggregate- and firm-level moments of asset prices and real quantities. Of particular significance, our two-factor model predicts the misspecification of CAPM. We demonstrate that the presence of higher marginal financing costs in the second half of the year not only facilitates the accurate matching of cash flow timing patterns but also effectively replicates the CAPM pricing errors that align with the observed data. In addition, we reproduce the YCC spread using our simulated data and provide cross-sectional characteristics at the portfolio level. In detail, we show that high YCC firms in the constrained subsample earn a higher return and also display a higher OCF spread, a lower return on assets (ROA), and higher cash savings, all consistent with our model intuition. This evidence on the YCC spread and associated relation with other firm decisions is also observed in the data.We then present empirical evidence supporting our model assumptions and implications in Section 5. First, we compare the risk exposure of the lowest YCC portfolio to that of the highest YCC portfolio to understand the cross-sectional variation in risk premiums. We show that firms with higher YCC are more exposed to aggregate TFP and issuance cost shocks. Using the generalized method of moments (GMM), we estimate the standard asset pricing equation and find that aggregate TFP and issuance cost shocks are significantly and positively priced in the cross-section of stock returns.6 Second, we use SDC platinum data to justify that financing costs are higher in the second half of the year, and confirm that unpaid revenues significantly impact linear financing costs. Additionally, we find that unpaid revenues have a greater impact on financing costs by year-end. The results support our assumptions that YCC is related to financial constraints and financial cost shocks. We also discuss alternative channels that may lead to YCC, such as tax reduction, managers’ compensation, and liquidity management; however, none of these channels explains the correlation between YCC and financial constraints. Further, we examine the ability of firm-level YCC to predict cross-sectional stock returns by using (Fama and MacBeth, 1973) regressions. The slope coefficient associated with a firm’s YCC is economically and statistically significant for the financially constrained subsample, but not for the unconstrained subsample. When we control for various firm characteristics that predict stock returns, we find that a one-unit-standard-deviation increase in the constrained firm’s YCC is associated with a 1.370% increase in the corresponding expected stock return.Finally, we verify that the positive YCC-return relation is not driven by other alternative channels. In detail, we conduct a comprehensive empirical analysis to test the validity of the YCC premium. Our results show that our YCC spread can still survive after we control for accrual effects in Sloan (1996), or for the cash-related premium in Palazzo (2012).Related literatureOur paper contributes to several strands of literature. First, our paper contributes to a well-developed literature that examines the dynamic implications of financial constraints on investment and cash holdings. Riddick and Whited (2009) and Bolton et al. (2011) suggest that firms hold cash because of financial constraints and cash flow shocks. Unlike previous studies, our paper focuses on endogenous cash collection decisions. Our findings also suggest that riskier firms save more cash, especially those exposed to both aggregate productivity and financial shocks. This finding is similar to that in Palazzo (2012), but our study extends the focus beyond aggregate productivity shocks.Our paper is also closely related to the literature that shows that equity financing costs are time varying. Warusawitharana and Whited (2015) and Bolton et al. (2013) suggest that equity financing costs vary because of mispricing. Eisfeldt and Muir (2016) focus on the role of time-varying aggregate financing costs. Our paper complements this literature by endogenously linking equity issuance costs to year-end cash collection decisions.Our paper also contributes to the trade credit and earnings management literature. Jones (1991), Dechow et al. (1995), and Leuz et al. (2003) show that higher unpaid revenue leads to higher agency problems, and that short sellers discipline firms from earnings management (Massa et al., 2015, Fang et al., 2016). Grigoris et al. (2023) document that trade-credit improves supplier-customer links and hedges aggregate searching costs for customers. Our paper complements these studies by quantifying the effect of unpaid revenue on aggregate equity issuance costs. More financially constrained firms have a greater incentive to decrease financing costs by increasing information transparency. This effect is widely recognized, but is nonetheless absent in the literature.Our paper contributes to the investment-based asset pricing literature as well, which explores the asset pricing implications of risk exposure to aggregate shocks (Zhang, 2005, Li, 2011, Belo and Lin, 2012, Eisfeldt and Papanikolaou, 2013, Belo et al., 2019, Lin et al., 2020). In addition, our paper contributes to the asset pricing literature that studies the implications of financial frictions for the cross-section of expected returns. Ai et al. (2020) finds that firms with low asset collateralizability are more financially constrained and riskier. Li and Tsou (2019) shows that the leased capital ratio measure is positively correlated with financial constraints and that firms with high leased capital are less risky. In contrast to these papers, our paper contributes to the literature by focusing on the implication of equity financing frictions on year-end cash collection, cash savings, and the cross-section of expected stock returns.Finally, our paper contributes to the extensive macroeconomics literature that studies the role of credit market frictions in generating fluctuations across business cycles (see Quadrini, 2011 and Brunnermeier et al., 2012 for recent reviews). The papers most closely related to ours are those emphasizing the importance of borrowing constraints and contract enforcements (Kiyotaki and Moore, 1997, Kiyotaki and Moore, 2019, Gertler and Kiyotaki, 2010, He and Krishnamurthy, 2013, Brunnermeier and Sannikov, 2014, Elenev et al., 2021). Gomes et al. (2015) study the asset pricing implications of credit market frictions in a production economy. In contrast to papers in this literature, our paper focuses on equity market frictions. Specifically, we allow firms to decrease equity financing costs by increasing their cash collection, which increases the information transparency of earnings and relaxes financial constraints.The rest of our paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we provide empirical facts about cash flow timing patterns and the relation between YCC and excess stock returns. In Section 3, we propose a rationale for our empirical observations and present a dynamic stochastic model in which a firm manages its cash collection, investments, and cash holdings. In Section 4, we discuss the calibration and simulation of our model to evaluate the way in which YCC explains firm risk exposure and firm characteristics. In Section 5, we provide more empirical evidence to support the assumption and implications of our model. In Section 6, we rule out alternative explanations, and we conclude this paper with Section 7.
article.2. Empirical facts: In this section, we present the prevalence of cash flow timing patterns resulting from YCC and their relation to financial constraints.2.1. DataOur main sample covers the period between 1988 and 2022. We begin with all US public firms for which we have accounting information from the Compustat Fundamental annual and quarterly databases. We use information on issuance fees from SDC Platinum database. Monthly stock returns are from the Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP). Asset pricing factors related to the (Fama and French, 2015) five-factor model and the (Carhart, 1997) four-factor model are downloaded from the data library of Kenneth French. We adopt the standard screening process for the CRSP/Compustat Merged Database. We exclude regulated utilities (SIC codes 4900-4999) and financial firms (SIC codes 6000-6999), and only keep domestic common shares trading on NYSE, Amex, or Nasdaq, older than 1 year since the IPO.7 Following Almeida et al. (2004), we remove firm-years where cash holdings exceeded total assets, or displayed asset or sales growth exceeding 100%. Additionally, we exclude firms with less than $1 million in physical capital and sales, and less than $5 million in market capitalization. We correct for potential delisting bias in stock returns following Shumway (1997).2.2. Measuring cash flows, earnings, and year-end cash collectionWe measure the year-end cash collection for firm i in year t as the decrease in noncash net working capital from fiscal Q2 to Q4 scaled by lagged total assets:8 (1)Y CCi,t=NWCi,tQ2−NWCi,tQ4ATi,t−1,in which NWCi,tQ2 and NWCi,tQ4 are the noncash net working capital of fiscal Q2 and Q4, respectively. The numerator of Y CCi,t, the change in noncash net working capital between fiscal Q2 and Q4, reflects the amount of collected cash near the end of the year.9 Higher Y CCi,t means more cash flows are collected by year-end, resulting in higher within-year volatility of cash flows.The adjustment of Y CCi,t can not only affect cash flow distribution within a year but also contributes to the difference between earnings and operating cash flows. Specifically, it is customary for firms to make sales and receive payments from customers at a later time, in which case earnings, currently in the form of working capital, are converted into cash flows in the future. Thus, the working capital management affects the mismatch between earnings recognition and cash payments, which drives an important difference between earnings and operating cash flows. We employ ROA (Compustat item IB) to measure a firm’s profitability according to Belo et al. (2017), and we use net cash receipts from operating activities (Compustat item OANCF + XINT) to construct operating cash flows following Lian and Ma (2020). Table B.1 reports details about the construction of variables.2.3. Cash flow timing patterns2.3.1. The prevalence of cash flow timing patternsThe literature suggests that unpaid revenue is associated with earnings management issues. Since year-end performance attracts more attention (Chae, 2005), more cash flows are collected at year-end (Frankel et al., 2017). Using US firm-level data, we find significant timing patterns of cash flows. Fig. 1 shows the time-series plot of semiannual average operating cash flows from 1987 to 2021. Firms receive more cash flows in the second half of the year relative to the first half. On average, firms received about 30% of their cash flows in the first half of the year, whereas they received almost 70% of their cash flows in the second half. Overall, we find strong cash flow timing patterns, which have been persistent for decades.Download: Download high-res image (303KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 1. Our results are robust with value- and equal-weighted average returns and different measures of financial constraints.2.5. The drivers of the YCC premiumIn this section, we discuss the factors driving the YCC premium. We begin by demonstrating that the within-year spread of inventories (INVT Q2-Q4) and total receivables (RECT Q2-Q4) are the primary drivers of the premium. We then show that equity financing shocks are associated with the within-year spread of inventories and total receivables. Finally, we show that the within-year timing patterns of INVT and RECT differ from the inventory growth spread in Belo et al. (2019) and from the trade credits spread in Grigoris et al. (2023).2.5.1. The components of YCCTo better understand the drivers of YCC premium, we decompose net working capital into five components: inventories (Compustat item INVT), total receivables (Compustat item RECT), other non-cash current assets (Compustat item ACO), accounts payable (Compustat item AP), and other current liabilities (Compustat item LCO). In Internet Appendix A.1, we report each component’s average within-year spread across five portfolios sorted on YCC in our financially constrained subsample, as measured by the WW index.11Panel A of Table A.1 in the Internet Appendix shows that inventories and total receivables account for about 50.5% of the cross-sectional variation in YCC. In addition, to test the return predictability of component(s) of YCC, we now sort portfolios based on each component of YCC and document their return spreads. Panel B of Table A.1 in the Internet Appendix shows that two of our five components – the within-year spread of inventories (INVT Q2-Q4) and total receivables (RECT Q2-Q4) – drive YCC and the YCC premium.122.5.2. Equity issuance cost shocks, inventory spread, and receivable spreadIn Section 2.4, we demonstrated that firms with financial constraints have higher YCC premium. In this subsection, we provide further support for the link between financial constraint shocks and YCC.First, we show that average inventory spread and receivable spread are related to financial constraints. In Table C.1 of the Internet Appendix, we demonstrate that average inventory spread and receivable spread are higher for financially constrained firms than for unconstrained firms. These results confirm that the within-year spread of inventories (INVT Q2-Q4) and total receivables (RECT Q2-Q4) drives the difference in YCC between constrained and unconstrained firms.Second, we show that the within-year spread of inventories and total receivables is associated with aggregate financial shocks. To measure aggregate financial shocks, we use issuance cost shocks (ICS) data from Belo et al. (2019). We then regress inventory spread and receivable spread on ICS, respectively. We report our regression results in Table C.2 of the Internet Appendix. The dependent variables in Columns (1), (2), and (3) are YCC, inventory spread (INVT Q2-Q4), and receivable spread (RECT Q2-Q4), respectively.13 We show that YCC, inventory spread, and receivable spread are negatively associated with ICS; that is, firms collect more cash flows by year-end when it is more costly to issue equity (low ICS).Overall, these results demonstrate that the within-year spread of inventories and receivables is related to financial constraints and financial constraint shocks.2.5.3. The YCC premium versus growth effect and counterparty risk premiumOne possible concern is that the YCC premium can be simply driven by growth effects, such as the asset growth effect (Cooper et al., 2008) and the inventory growth effect (Belo and Lin, 2012), both of which are closely related to YCC and negatively predict stock returns. Thus, it is important to differentiate YCC from inventory growth (IG hereafter) and asset growth (AG hereafter).By definition, IG and AG are measured at different frequencies compared to YCC. Specifically, IG and AG measure the annual growth of inventories (INV Ti,tQ4−INV Ti,t−1Q4) and assets (ATi,tQ4−ATi,t−1Q4), respectively. Different from YCC, IG and AG do not include information on early-year decisions (i.e., decisions by fiscal Q2). If the YCC measured with a quarterly frequency (Q2−Q4) is simply driven by growth effects measured with a yearly frequency, we should then expect the other decreases in NWC within a year to also predict future returns (e.g., Q1-Q2, Q3-Q4). Panel A of Table C.3 in the Internet Appendix shows that the return spread is statistically significant when we sort portfolios based on the difference in NWC between the first half of the year and the second half of the year. However, the return spread diminishes if we sort portfolios based on other changes in NWC (i.e., Q3 - Q4 and Q1 - Q2.14). Therefore, we conclude that the return predictability of YCC is not simply driven by growth effects.To ensure robustness, we also propose a new measure: Y CCnew, which is measured as NWCi,tQ2ATi,tQ2−NWCi,tQ4ATi,tQ4. This new measure captures the decrease in the proportion of net working capital from fiscal Q2 to fiscal Q4, but is no longer negatively related to asset growth.15 Table C.4 of the Internet Appendix shows that this new measure of YCC positively predicts future returns very well.In addition, the literature suggests that IG contains information about business cycles (Belo and Lin, 2012) while account receivables contain information about production network risks. However, we show that YCC contains information about financing frictions. Thus, our task is to examine whether a firm’s exposure to aggregate financial shocks also helps explain the cross-sectional variation in average returns of portfolios sorted by IG and R/S. Panel A of Table C.5 in the Internet Appendix reports risk characteristics for the five portfolios sorted on IG. We show that the sensitivities to ICS between the highest and the lowest IG quintile portfolios are not statistically different, with a t-statistic of 1.34. Panel B of Table C.5 in the Internet Appendix shows that the counterparty risks cannot be explained by aggregate financial shocks because the sensitivities to ICS between the highest and the lowest R/S quintile portfolios are not statistically different, with a t-statistic of 1.38. Our results suggest that IG and R/S do not likely contain information about financial cost shocks.To further confirm the novelty of the YCC premium, we examine whether the YCC premium still exists after we control for IG or R/S. To do so, we implement double-sort analyses. We first control for IG and construct the YCC spread within each IG-sorted portfolio. Our results when we control for IG are reported in Panel A of Table C.6 in the Internet Appendix. We show that YCC spreads are 7.91% and 6.06% per year among medium and high IG firms, respectively. The YCC spreads within these two IG-sorted portfolios are significant at the 1% level, and the joint test suggests that the average YCC spread of all IG portfolios is significantly positive at the 1% level. In Panel B, the joint test suggests that the average IG spread in our sample is not significant.We then control for R/S and construct the YCC spread within each R/S-sorted portfolio. Panel A of Table C.7 in the Internet Appendix shows that the YCC spread is 5.97% and 9.59% in low and high R/S-sorted groups, respectively; both results are significant above 5% level. Moreover, the YCC spread is jointly significant across the three R/S-sorted portfolios, with a p-value of less than 0.01. However, Panel B of Table C.7 in the Internet Appendix shows that the counterparty premium in our sample is insignificant in all YCC-sorted portfolios. Our findings suggest that the YCC premium cannot be explained by IG and R/S effects.Finally, as introduced in Internet Appendix A.1, within-year spread of inventories and receivables are main drivers of the YCC premium. Therefore, we further investigate the extent to which the variation in the average returns of the YCC-sorted portfolios can be explained by the IG premium in Belo and Lin (2012), as well as by the counterparty premium in Grigoris et al. (2023); to do so, we perform standard asset pricing tests. Specifically, we run time-series regressions of YCC-sorted portfolios’ excess returns on the (Fama and French, 2015) five-factor model, the average returns of the long-short portfolio sorted on IG, and the average returns of the long-short portfolio sorted on R/S. As shown in Table C.8 of the Internet Appendix, the risk-adjusted return (intercept) of the YCC-sorted high-minus-low portfolio remains large and statistically significant at the 1% level. Additionally, the alpha in this model remains comparable to the YCC spread (i.e., the return on the high-minus-low portfolio) in our univariate sorting (Table 3). In sum, our results from asset pricing tests in Table C.8 of the Internet Appendix suggest that the cross-sectional return spread across portfolios sorted on YCC cannot be explained by either the IG premium or counterparty premium.
article.3. Dynamic investment-based model: To rationalize our empirical findings in Section 2, we build a dynamic structural model of investment, cash holdings, cash collection, and firm financing at a semiannual frequency.We first specify a firm’s production and capital investment processes. Next, we discuss the timing of cash collection and compare the definitions of earnings and cash flows. We then introduce the link between cash collection and equity financing costs. Finally, we discuss the firm’s problem and asset pricing implications.3.1. ProductionFirms use physical capital (Kt) to produce output (Πt). For brevity’s sake, we omit firm index i whenever possible. The production function is given by: (2)Πt=ezt+xtKtα,in which zt and xt are the level of the firm’s idiosyncratic and aggregate productivity, respectively. The term Kt is physical capital, and α∈(0,1) captures a decreasing returns-to-scale technology. Following Lin and Zhang (2013), the level of the firm’s productivity is the sum of an idiosyncratic shock zt, and an aggregate shock xt, and both the idiosyncratic shock zt and aggregate shock xt follow an AR(1) process: (3)zt+1=ρzzt+ϵz,t+1, (4)xt+1=x¯(1−ρx)+ρxxt+ϵx,t+1,in which ϵz,t+1 and ϵx,t+1 are firm-specific and aggregate productivity shocks, respectively. They are normally distributed with a zero mean and standard deviation σz and σx. ρz and ρx are the autocorrelation of firm-specific and aggregate productivity shocks, respectively, and x¯ is the long-run average aggregate productivity, which determines the long-run average scale of the economy.16Physical capital evolves according to the standard law of motion: (5)Kt+1=It+(1−δ)Kt,in which It represents investment, and δ denotes the depreciation rate of physical capital. A firm’s purchase or sale of capital incurs convex adjustment costs, given by: (6)ϕ(It,Kt)=gIt22Kt,in which g determines the slope of the marginal adjustment cost and is greater than zero.3.2. Demand for cash flow paymentsIn the model, products can be transferred into paid revenue or unpaid revenue: (7)Πt=λtΠt︸Paid revenue+(1−λt)Πt︸Unpaid revenue,in which λt is the proportion of products paid with cash at time t, which ranges from 0 to 1. λtΠt is the paid revenue, which can generate immediate payments. To obtain revenue in cash immediately, firms (suppliers) must offer a discounted price pd to customers. We note that pd should be smaller than 11+rf; otherwise, customers have no incentive to pay in cash immediately. (1−λt)Πt is the revenue that is not paid at time t but will be collected in the next period. In our model, the unpaid revenue at time t can be considered net working capital in the form of accounts receivable or inventories.17 Thus, according to cash flow-based accounting, a firm’s operating cash flows (OCF) at time t are:18 (8)OCFt=(1−τ)[(1−λt−1)Πt−1+pdλtΠt−f+rsLt]+τδKt.In our model, the definition of OCF can be clearly mapped to accounting definitions which we discuss in detail in Internet Appendix A.2. According to the definition, OCF contains unpaid revenue at time t−1, (1−λt−1)Πt−1, and paid revenue at time t, pdλtΠt. f is the operating cost, and rsLt is the interest from saving cash Lt. rs is the cash saving rate. Following the literature, we assume rs=rf−κ, in which κ is the constant wedge between the risk-free and saving rates. κ is greater than zero, such that cash holdings are costly to firms (i.e., rs<rf); otherwise, firms with financial frictions would strictly prefer to reinvest profits in cash assets. The last term, τδKt, is the tax deduction of depreciation.According to accrual-based accounting, a firm’s earnings at time t are:19 (9)Earningst=(1−τ)[(1−λt)Πt+pdλtΠt−f+rsLt]+τδKt.Earnings are defined as the sum of operating cash flows (OCF) and revenue accruals.20 (1−λt)Πt+pdλtΠt is the gross profit at time t. Other items are the same as those in the OCF equation.3.3. Payout, monitoring, and financingA firm must finance its capital investment, adjustment costs, and cash reserves with cash, cash flow, and equity financing. The firm’s budget constraint can be written as: (10)Et=OCFt−It−ϕt−(Lt+1−Lt).The first term is the operating cash flow defined in Eq. (8). It and ϕt are the investment in physical capital and incurred capital adjustment costs, respectively. The last term Lt+1−Lt represents the investment in cash holdings at time t. The firm’s cash holdings are subject to non-negative constraint, Lt+1≥0.If Et is greater than zero, then the firm pays out; if Et is smaller than zero, then the firm raises external financing, which is more costly than cash financing. In particular, we assume that equity issuance costs take the following form: (11)Ψ(Et,λt,st)=1Et<0{a0Kt−[a1+aλst(1−λt)]Et}ξt−a2,in which 1Et<0 is an indicator function that is one if Et is smaller than zero, suggesting that the firm issues equity. a0 captures the fixed cost of accessing the market. a1 is the constant linear cost of equity issuance. We then assume that agency costs are determined by two components: (i) the proportion of unpaid revenue and (ii) monitors’ attention. The literature suggests that unpaid revenue increases information asymmetry (Iliev, 2010, Massa et al., 2015, Fang et al., 2016) and results in higher financing costs. In addition, monitors’ attention to cash flow payments within a fiscal year is asymmetric. According to the literature, monitors pay greater attention to fiscal year-end performance (Brown and Pinello, 2007, Fan et al., 2010, Frankel et al., 2017). To model the timing patterns, we consider a deterministic regime shift in aλst between two different states (st∈{L,H}): aλL in the first half of the year and aλH in the second half of the year, in which 0<aλL<aλH. For each state, it switches to the other in the next period. These findings, taken together, indicate that the agency cost is aλst(1−λt)Et. Following Belo et al. (2019), the financing cost is also affected by the aggregate financial shock ξt and the issuance cost sensitivity a2. The aggregate financial shock follows an AR(1) process: (12)ln(ξt+1)=(1−ρξ)ξ̄+ρξln(ξt)+ϵξ,t+1.We assume that the unconditional expectation of the aggregate financial shock is one.21 ϵξ,t+1 is the aggregate shock to the financing cost, which is normally distributed with a zero mean and standard deviation σξ.Finally, the effective cash flow Dt distributed to shareholders is given by: (13)Dt=Et−Ψ(Et,λt,st).Year-end cash collection behaviorAssuming that t denotes the first half of a given year and t+1 denotes the second half, the year-end cash collection behavior can be expressed as the difference in unpaid revenue between the first and second half of the year, (1−τ)((1−λt)Πt−(1−λt+1)Πt+1). Because year-end attention is higher than attention at the beginning of the year, firms have a greater incentive to decrease their working capital by offering less trade credit to customers at year-end compared to the beginning of the year. If a firm collects more cash flows at year-end, then the year-end collection is larger than 0 ((1−τ)((1−λt)Πt−(1−λt+1)Πt+1)>0). Higher year-end collection suggests that more cash payments are collected by year-end.3.4. Firm’s problemA firm takes the stochastic discount factor (SDF) to value cash flows in period t+1, Mt,t+1 as given, and we specify SDF as a function of the two aggregate shocks: (14)Mt,t+1=11+rfexp(−γxΔln(xt+1)−γξΔln(ξt+1))Et[exp(−γxΔln(xt+1)−γξΔln(ξt+1))].in which γx and γξ are the market price of risk parameters with a positive sign, meaning that a negative shock from productivity (lower xt+1) or financing costs (lower ξt+1) results in higher marginal utility. We note that we have normalized the SDF so that the risk-free rate is always equal to rf.We define Vt(zt,ξt,st,Kt,Lt,λt−1,Πt−1) as the cum-dividend market value. Firms solve the maximization problem by making capital investment, cash savings, and cash collection decisions: (15)Vt(xt,ξt,zt,st,Kt,Lt,λt−1,Πt−1)=maxKt+1,Lt+1,λtDt+Et[Mt,t+1Vt+1(xt+1,ξt+1,st+1,zt+1,Kt+1,Lt+1,λt,Πt)].Appendix A reports the detailed procedure we use to solve the model via value function iteration. In the following subsections, we solve the firm’s optimal cash collection (Section 3.4.1) and cash savings (Section 3.4.2) policies and give our intuition behind them.3.4.1. Optimal cash payment and the timing patternsThe optimal interior solution with respect to cash payment (λt) is: (16)(1−τ)pdΠt−∂Ψ(Et,λt,st)∂λt=EtMt,t+1(1−τ)Πt+∂Ψ(Et+1,λt+1,st+1)∂λt, in which ∂Ψ(Et,λt,st)∂λt=1Et<0{aλstEt−[a1+aλst(1−λt)](1−τ)pdΠt}ξt−a2, and ∂Ψ(Et+1,λt+1,st+1)∂λt=1Et+1<0{[a1+aλst+1(1−λt+1)](1−τ)Πt}ξt+1−a2. The Euler equation sets the marginal benefit of increasing cash payments at time t equal to the expected marginal discounted cost of increasing cash payments. The left-hand side of Eq. (16) is the marginal benefit of increasing one unit percentage of λt at time t, which can be interpreted as the marginal increase in payouts (i.e., (1−τ)pdΠt) plus the marginal decrease in financing costs (i.e., −∂Ψ(Et,λt)∂λt), which is caused by higher information transparency and lower external financing. The right-hand side of Eq. (16) is the expected marginal cost of increasing one unit of λt at time t, including the expected marginal decrease in the dividend payment and the increase in future equity issuance costs.A firm’s cash collection decision is determined by the discount cost and external financing costs. First, a firm with a high discount cost (low pd) has less of an incentive to collect cash because it is less likely to offer costly discounted prices to its customers, all else equal. Second, a firm’s cash collection can also be decided through the external financing channel. The more external financing there is, the more beneficial increasing cash payments would become. As a result, firms with more external financing tend to collect more cash flows at time t to substitute for external financing. In addition, increasing λt means that fewer earnings are carried over to the next period, which results in lower cash flows and higher expected financing costs at time t+1. Collectively, all else equal, firms with higher current financing needs tend to increase λt to lower their external financing costs. This increase in λt continues until the marginal value equals the expected marginal cost of increasing one unit percentage of λt.Furthermore, the external financing channel drives the year-end cash collection in our model. Specifically, when a firm is more disciplined (i.e., at the end of the year), it is more inclined to increase cash payments because the marginal benefits of increasing λ are high (aλst=aλH)), whereas the marginal costs of increasing λ are low. However, those related benefits and costs are totally reversed in the next period, resulting in a higher ratio of unpaid revenue at the beginning of the year. In Section 3.4.2, we explore more deeply the relationship between the year-end cash collection decision, the cash decision, and financial constraints.3.4.2. Optimal cash holdingsIn this section, we discuss the optimal cash policy by analyzing the first-order condition of cash holdings. We then introduce two channels that affect the marginal value of cash. Finally, we establish the relationship between financial constraints and the marginal value of cash. Given the firm’s optimization problem, the interior optimal cash policy should satisfy the following condition: (17)1−ΨEt′(Et,λt,st)=EtMt,t+1(1+(1−τ)rs)(1−ΨEt+1′(Et+1,λt+1,st+1)).The left-hand side of the equation is the marginal cost of increasing one unit of cash, the forgone dividend, and the marginal financing cost at time t. The right-hand side of the equation is the marginal benefit of increasing one unit of cash at time t+1: the taxed interest rate plus the expected marginal reduction in the equity issuance cost.Cash savings and aggregate riskWe can rewrite the right-hand side of Eq. (17) as (18)EtMt,t+1(1+(1−τ)rs)(1−ΨEt+1′(Et+1,λt+1,st+1))=(1+(1−τ)rs){(1−Et[ΨEt+1′(Et+1,λt+1,st+1)])/(1+rf)+Cov(Mt,t+1,−ΨEt+1′(Et+1,λt+1,st+1))}.According to Riddick and Whited (2009) and Eisfeldt and Rampini (2009), under risk-neutrality, the covariance term disappears from Eq. (18), and risk plays no role in determining the firm’s optimal savings policy: 1−ΨEt′(Et,λt,st)=1+(1−τ)rs1+rf(1−Et[ΨEt+1′(Et+1,λt+1,st+1)]).This equation highlights several aspects of our intuition. First, cash derives value because it serves as an alternative to costly external financing and thus provides more financial flexibility in the future. Indeed, a firm optimally holds no cash if external financing is free. Second, the term (1−τ)rs<rf indicates that carrying cash holdings is costly for a firm because the interest is taxed and the interest rate is also lower than the risk-free rate. Thus, an optimal interior cash policy balances the flexibility benefit with the carrying cost.However, in our model, the marginal value of cash is decided by two effects: the precautionary motive and risk consideration. First, the precautionary motive is reflected by the expected marginal financing costs, −Et[ΨEt+1′(Et+1,λt+1,st+1)], in Eq. (18). Specifically, firms with higher expected marginal financing costs would undertake more financing costs in the future and thus would hold more cash to alleviate future financial difficulties. Second, the risk consideration is represented by the positive covariance term in Eq. (18). When the aggregate financial shock is positively priced, firms will save more cash because the expected marginal financing costs covary positively with the discount factor. In detail, a negative aggregate financial shock would result in higher expected marginal financing costs and a higher discount factor. In our model, due to the covariance term, financially constrained firms measured with the marginal value of cash would suffer more from aggregate financial shocks than in the risk-neutrality model and thus would save more cash.Marginal value of cash and financial constraintsFollowing Bolton et al. (2011), the marginal value of cash is generally interpreted as capturing how financially constrained a firm is. The intuition is that cash is valuable for constrained firms. Firms respond to financial constraints by optimally managing their cash holdings so as to avoid situations of financial distress. According to Bolton et al. (2011), if the marginal value of cash is equal to or less than one, then a firm does not suffer from financial friction and thus is considered financially unconstrained. However, if the marginal value of cash is greater than one, then the firm has difficulties obtaining funds because of the external financing costs. For interior solutions, the marginal value of cash is equal to 1−ΨE′(Et,λt,st)=1−1Et<0{−[a1+aλst(1−λt)]}ξt−a2,22 which implies that in our model, financially constrained firms defined by the marginal value of cash must pursue external financing (Et<0) for interior solutions.3.4.3. Financial constraints, year-end cash collection, and cash holdingsThis section discusses three important implications of the relationship between financial constraints, year-end cash collection, and cash holdings.Year-end cash collection and cash timing patternsWe find that year-end cash collection drives cash timing patterns. To explore this relationship, we combine Eq. (16) and Eq. (17) as follows: (19)pd−∂Ψ(Et,λt,st)∂λt/[(1−τ)Πt]=11+(1−τ)rs−ΨEt′(Et,λt,st)/[1+(1−τ)rs].The left- and right-hand sides of Eq. (19) are the standardized forms of the left-hand sides of Eq. (16) and Eq. (17), respectively.23 Despite the change in magnitude, we still recognize the left-hand side of Eq. (19) as the marginal cost of increasing unpaid revenue and the right-hand side of Eq. (19) as the marginal cost of holding cash. This equation shows that a firm faces a trade-off between choosing to hold cash or increasing unpaid revenue. Such a firm would adjust the amount of unpaid revenue until the cost of holding unpaid revenue is equal to the cost of holding cash; as a result, year-end cash collection can result in cash timing patterns. In particular, when the cost of increasing unpaid revenue is low at the beginning of the year, the firm would be more likely to substitute unpaid revenue for cash holdings. In contrast, at year-end when the cost of increasing unpaid revenue is high, then the firm tends to substitute cash holdings for unpaid revenue. In other words, unpaid revenue and cash are substitutes, since both serve as a buffer against future cash flow shortfalls and thus increase future financial flexibility.Financial constraints and year-end cash collectionAdditionally, there is a positive relationship between year-end cash collection and financial constraints. For the interior solutions with respect to cash holdings, if Et<0, then the firm is regarded as financially constrained because its marginal value of cash is greater than one. Because of its current financing needs (Et<0) and potential future financial frictions indicated by its high marginal value of cash, a financially constrained firm is more affected by the external financing channel. In particular, financially constrained firms are more likely to pursue external financing, which activates the terms ∂Ψ(Et,λt,st)∂λt and ∂Ψ(Et+1,λt+1,st+1)∂λt in Eq. (16). Therefore, they are more affected by regime-shifting attention (aλst), which drives year-end cash collection. However, if Et≥0, then the firm is considered to be financially unconstrained. It does not have many financing needs – either currently or in the near future – and thus is less affected by the external financing channel.Year-end cash collection and cash savingsFinally, we can observe a positive relationship between year-end cash collection and cash savings for two reasons. First, as we discussed earlier, year-end cash collection and cash savings are both positively affected by financial constraints. Second, high year-end cash collection results in more external financing in the future (lower Et+1), which further contributes to expected marginal financing costs and the positive covariance term in Eq. (18).3.5. Asset pricing implicationsIn the model, firms make optimal capital investment, cash holdings, and cash collection decisions. Risk and expected stock returns are determined endogenously. To make the link explicitly, we can evaluate the value function in Eq. (15) at the optimum and obtain: (20)Vt=Dt+Et[Mt,t+1Vt+1]⇒0=Et[Mt,t+1rt+1e],in which rt+1e is the stock excess return, equaling Vt+1/[Vt−Dt]−(1+rf). With some algebra, Eq. (20) can be rewritten as: (21)Et[rt+1e]=γx×Covt(rt+1e,Δln(xt+1))+γξ×Covt(rt+1e,Δln(ξt+1))=βx,tγxσx2+βξ,tγξσξ2.The equilibrium risk premiums in the model are determined by the endogenous covariances of a firm’s excess stock returns with the two aggregate shocks (quantity of risk) and by the market price of the two risk parameters (γx and γξ) in Eq. (14). The pre-specified positive sign of the market prices of risk implies that, all else equal, assets with returns that have a more positive covariance with the aggregate productivity shock are riskier and offer higher average returns in equilibrium. Similarly, all else equal, assets with returns that have a more positive covariance with the aggregate financial shock are riskier and offer higher average returns in equilibrium. In Section 3.6, we explain in detail the economic mechanism driving the YCC premium.3.6. The mechanismTo explain the sign of the year-end cash collection premium in the model, we then describe how each of the priced shocks, ϵx and ϵξ, respectively, contributes to the spread.3.6.1. The contribution of ϵxAs we discussed earlier, the risk exposure to aggregate productivity shocks is affected by sales paid with cash, pdλtΠt. As a result, all else equal, firms that collect more cash flows (higher λ) at year-end are more exposed to aggregate productivity shocks. That is, the increase in aggregate productivity (ϵx>0) has a larger percentage of positive change on the operating cash flows of the high YCC firm relative to the low YCC firm. Therefore, βx,tYCC=high>βx,tYCC=low>0.3.6.2. The contribution of ϵξThe risk exposure to aggregate financial shocks is largely determined by the extent of external financing. Firms with high year-end cash collection do not carry over much of their earnings to the next period, resulting in more external financing (lower Et+1). However, firms with low year-end cash collection have more cash flows in the next period and thus a low need for equity issuance. If there is a negative financial shock on all firms (ϵξ<0), then high YCC firms would be more negatively affected since they are more likely to pay the costs of accessing the equity market, implying βξ,tYCC=high>βξ,tYCC=Low>0.These findings, taken together, indicate that according to Eq. (21), the risk premium between the high and low year-end cash collection portfolio is: (22)Et[rt+1e]YCC=high−Et[rt+1e]YCC=low=(βx,tYCC=high−βx,tYCC=Low)︸(+)γxσx2+(βξ,tYCC=high−βξ,tYCC=Low)︸(+)γξσξ2.In sum, the risk premium is positive.
article.4. Quantitative model implications: In this section, we calibrate our model parameters and solve the model numerically. We then investigate the model’s quantitative predictions and discuss the targeted moments. Finally, we examine the mechanisms of the YCC premium.4.1. CalibrationTable 4 shows the calibration of our model parameters. We carefully discuss the aggregate and firm-level moments targeted by our calibration. To quantitatively evaluate the calibration of the model parameters, we compare a wide set of targeted moments and nontargeted moments with those in the data in Table 5. To obtain the model-implied moments, we simulate the policy path for 5000 firms with different productivity shock paths for 1200 periods (600 years) at a semiannual frequency. We then drop the first 1000 periods to neutralize the impact of the initial condition, and we treat the remaining simulated data for 200 periods similarly to those from the economy’s stationary distribution. Finally, we simulate 25 samples and report our cross-sample average results as model moments. Panel A of Table 5 reports targeted moments in both the data and the model.Firm’s technology: general parameters. We set the curvature of the production function α to be 0.58, close to Cooper and Haltiwanger (2006). The semiannual depreciation rate, δ, is set to be 0.01×6, as in Bloom (2009). We set the corporate tax rate to be 0.35, consistent with Hennessy and Whited, 2005, Hennessy and Whited, 2007.Table 4. Calibration.ParametersSymbolValueTechnologyReturns to scaleα0.58Corporate tax rateτ0.35Rate of depreciation for capitalδ0.06Quadratic capital adjustment costg0.26Operating costf0.05Fixed financing costa0×100.01Linear financing costa10.06Issuance cost sensitivitya210.00Agency cost highaλH0.29Agency cost lowaλL0.05Discounted pricepd0.95Carrying costκ0.016/2Stochastic processPersistence of idiosyncratic productivity shockρz0.81STD of idiosyncratic productivity shockσz0.20Persistence of aggregate productivity shockρx0.88STD of aggregate productivity shockσx0.07Persistence of aggregate financial shockρξ0.87STD of aggregate financial shockσξ0.04SDFSemiannual risk free raterf2.1%/2Risk aversion to productivity shockγx6Risk aversion to financing shockγξ16This table presents the calibrated parameter values of the baseline model. The model is calibrated at the semiannual frequency.Firm’s technology: costs. We calibrate the quadratic capital adjustment cost, g, to be 0.26, so as to match the volatility and interdecile range of firm-level investment rates (0.17 and 0.34 in the data; 0.14 and 0.26 in the model).24 The assumed operating cost largely determines firms’ cash flows. Thus, we set f to be 0.05, which implies an average ratio of cash flows to assets of 0.17, close to the data at 0.16. We set the traditional equity issuance cost parameters to be a0=0.01 and a1=0.06, which imply that the average fraction of equity issuance is 23% (22% in the data) and that the mean of the firm-level issuance-to-asset ratio is 0.02 (0.02 in the data).25 In addition, those equity financing cost parameters suggest that the fixed cost of equity issuance is around 1% of total proceeds and that the variable equity issuance cost is less than 10% of the amount of issuance, which is similar to the estimates in Altınkılıç and Hansen (2000) and Hennessy and Whited (2007). The issuance cost sensitivity parameter a2 is calibrated to be 10 to match the volatility of the ratio of firm-level issuance to assets (0.04 in the data and 0.06 in the model). The Compustat variable SSTK contains the exercise of stock options to employees as a form of compensation (among other forms) and, as documented in Fama and French (2005) and McKeon (2015), these passive issuances are large, relative to SEOs and private placements. Following Denis and McKeon (2021), when culculating the fraction of equity issuance, we use the 3% threshold on market value to purge the vast majority of passive equity issuances from our Compustat data without sacrificing a substantial number of active issuances. We set pd to be 0.95.26 The carrying cost of holding cash κ is set to be 0.016/2 to match the average ratio of net working capital (0.06 in the data and 0.08 in the model).Finally, we set aλH=0.29 and aλL=0.05. aλH and aλL are essential for targeting the timing patterns and determining the costs of holding unpaid revenue. On the one side, we successfully match the average year-end cash collection (0.01 in the data and 0.01 in the model). On the other side, cash flow timing patterns simultaneously generate a sizable YCC premium that cannot be fully explained by CAPM, and our model is a two-factor asset pricing model, which directly predicts the misspecification of CAPM. Therefore, we can utilize the failure of CAPM (CAPM alpha) to identify these time-varying equity issuance costs, which helps us pin down CAPM pricing errors (CAPM alpha) at 5.66% in the model, close to the data moment at 4.92%.27Stochastic process. The persistence of idiosyncratic and aggregate productivity shocks, ρz and ρx, are calibrated to be 0.81 and 0.88 to match the autocorrelation of the ratio of firm-level cash flows to assets and the ratio of aggregate cash flows to assets (0.46 and 0.88 in the data, and 0.64 and 0.70 in the model). We set the conditional volatility of both idiosyncratic and aggregate productivity shocks to be σz=0.20 and σx=0.07 so as to match the volatility of the ratio of firm-level cash flows to assets and the ratio of aggregate cash flows to assets. Given the volatility and autocorrelation of the ratio of aggregate equity issuance to assets in the data (0.01 and 0.53), we set the conditional volatility of the aggregate issuance cost shock to be σξ=0.04 and set the persistence of the aggregate issuance cost shock to be ρξ=0.87, implying that the volatility and autocorrelation of the ratio of aggregate issuance to assets are 0.02 and 0.43 in the model, respectively.SDF. We set the semiannual risk-free rate to be 2.1%/2. Market price of macroeconomic shocksIn this section, we show that firms’ different degrees of exposure to macroeconomic shocks help explain cross-sectional variation in the risk premiums of YCC-sorted portfolios. We undertake this analysis by assuming that the SDF that prices all assets in the economy is given by: (23)Mt=1−bMKT×MKTt−b′Macrot.Here, consistent with our model, we introduce two different macroeconomic shocks: Macrot, which is a 2 × 1 column vector that contains the proxies for aggregate financial and productivity shocks; and b, which is a 2 × 1 column vector of the corresponding market price of risk parameters. Specifically, we use issuance cost shock (ICS) data from Belo et al. (2019) to measure the aggregate financial shock, and we use the log difference in total factor productivity (TFP) from the Federal Reserve Bank’s official website to measure the aggregate productivity shock. For the sake of comparison, we also consider the standard capital asset pricing model (CAPM), which includes only the stock market factor (MKT). We then estimate the market price of risk parameters by two-step GMM using the standard asset pricing moment condition E[ri,teMt]=0, in which ri,te is the excess return on portfolio i at time t. Finally, we compute the sum of squared errors (SSQE), mean absolute pricing errors (MAPE), and the J-statistic of the overidentifying restrictions of model. The differences in sensitivities between the highest and the lowest portfolio are significantly positive with t-statistics of 1.72 and 3.10, respectively. State-dependent financing costsIn our model, cash flow timing patterns are mainly driven by regime-shifting financing costs. To justify this assumption, we collect detailed issuance information from the SDC Platinum database. We then merge the SDC Platinum database with the fundamental information from Compustat. Following the literature (Piotroski and So, 2012), we match issuance details and the most recently available fundamental information by imposing a four-month information lag on fundamentals. Finally, we use linear regressions to empirically examine the state-dependent impact of unpaid revenue on equity financing costs.The dependent variable represents total issuance fees scaled by assets. Following the literature (Altınkılıç and Hansen, 2000, Fang, 2005), we also control for standard firm-level variables commonly found in empirical studies: total proceeds (the ratio of proceeds to assets), firm size (log of total assets), financial leverage, return volatility (annualized standard deviation of monthly stock returns), and investment opportunities (market-to-book). We also include the firm fixed effects to account for unobserved factors.To examine the impact of unpaid revenue on the linear term of financing costs, we include the interaction term between total proceeds and the dummy of high unpaid revenue (Proceeds×DummyUnpaid) in column (1) of Table 9. The dummy variable DummyUnpaid is one if a firm’s unpaid revenue ratio is above the median, otherwise zero. Consistent with the model, we measure the unpaid revenue ratio as the ratio of net working capital to sales. We find that the interaction term is significantly positive at the 1% level, while the coefficient of unpaid revenue is not statistically different from zero. This implies that the direct financing costs of firms with high unpaid revenue are higher. In column (2) of Table 9, we also introduce an interaction term between total proceeds, high unpaid revenue dummy, and a dummy variable, DummyQ3,Q4, which equals one for all fiscal Q3 and Q4 and zero for fiscal Q1 and Q2. More importantly, the coefficient of the interaction term, Proceeds×DummyUnpaid×DummyQ3,Q4, is 0.007 and statistically significant. The coefficient of the interaction term suggests that firms with high unpaid revenue face 0.7% higher year-end equity issuance costs. These results are consistent with our assumption that marginal equity financing costs are more sensitive to year-end unpaid revenue.Table 9. While the empirical evidence presented in this section supports the financing cost channel in explaining the correlation between YCC and financial constraints, there are alternative explanations for this correlation. We consider three possible channels—namely, tax planning, managerial compensation, and liquidity management. These channels may be related to YCC and financial constraints. To rule out alternative explanations, we provide a detailed discussion of these channels and examine whether these channels are correlated to financial constraints in Internet Appendix B. We show that none of these channels helps explain the link between YCC and financial constraints.5.3. On the one hand, the ROA spread is close to zero across all five portfolios – much smaller than the OCF spread, 0.099, in the highest YCC portfolio – implying that the timing pattern of operating cash flows might not result from earnings manipulations. On the other hand, we find a positive I/A spread in all portfolios, and there is not much variation in the I/A spread. One possible explanation for our finding is that the I/A spread could be driven by other factors, such as tax minimization (Kinney and Trezevant, 1993, Xu and Zwick, 2020).Overall, our model implications on YCC and firm characteristics can be confirmed empirically.
article.6. Alternative channels: Our model suggests that a primary explanation for the year-end cash collection premium is that firms with high year-end cash collections are more financially constrained and thus more exposed to aggregate financial shocks. Although our model suggests that this mechanism is quantitatively important in Section 3.6, it is still possible that the return spread is related to other mechanisms. Thus, in this section, we consider some alternative explanations and examine their impacts on the year-end cash collection premium.6.1. The YCC premium versus the accruals effectSloan (1996) shows that firms with low accruals earn high future returns. Since the change in noncash net working capital is also a component of accruals, there is a mechanical relation between accruals and YCC. Following Sloan (1996), we define the accruals (denoted ACC) of firm i at time t as: (25)ACCi,t=NWCi,tQ4−NWCi,t−1Q4−DPi,tATi,t−1,in which DPi,t is the depreciation of firm i at time t. There are some differences between the definitions of accruals and YCC. First, compared with the definition of YCC in Eq. (1), the construction of accruals includes a depreciation term; we do not use depreciation when computing YCC because depreciation, as a tool of earnings management, would not affect cash flow items. Second, with respect to time span, ACC is calculated as the increase in noncash net working capital for the entire fiscal year, whereas YCC is constructed as the decrease in noncash net working capital in the second half of the fiscal year, resulting in a negative correlation between ACC and YCC. Since there is a large literature on the accrual anomaly that suggests firms with high accruals earn a low return (see, e.g., Sloan, 1996, Hirshleifer et al., 2011), it is important to examine whether the year-end cash collection premium still exists after we control for accruals. To do this, we implement a double-sort analysis following Grigoris et al. (2023). First, we construct the YCC spread across three accruals-sorted portfolios. Our results in Panel A of Table C.9 in the Internet Appendix show that the YCC spread earns over 6.7% per year among high accrual firms. The YCC spread within this accruals-sorted portfolios is significant at the 1% level. Furthermore, a joint test shows that the average YCC premium across all accrual groups is significant at the 5% level. In sum, our findings suggest that the YCC spread cannot be explained by the accruals effect of Sloan (1996).In Panel B of Table C.9 in the Internet Appendix, we reverse the order of the sorts to examine whether the accruals effect survives when we control for YCC. Our results show that the accruals spread are statistically significant for the portfolio of low YCC firms. However, the null hypothesis that the accruals spread is zero across all three YCC-sorted portfolios is also rejected. The economic determinants of the YCC premium may not help to explain the accruals effect of Sloan (1996). In summary, the ACC and YCC premium cannot explain each other.6.2. The YCC premium versus the cash-related premiumPalazzo (2012) proposes that riskier firms (i.e., firms with a higher correlation between cash flow and the aggregate shock) have higher financing needs and thus have higher optimal savings. This precautionary savings motive implies a positive relation between excess equity returns and cash holdings. Since our measure of YCC also varies positively with cash savings as well as cash holdings, we must show whether our cross-sectional results are driven by the cash-related premium.As previously, we conduct a double-sort analysis in Table C.10 of the Internet Appendix. In Panel A, we show that the YCC spread is statistically significant in the medium and high cash-sorted portfolios, and the joint test suggests that the average YCC spread of all cash-sorted portfolios is significantly positive at the 1% level. In Panel B, the cash spread is insignificant across all cash-sorted portfolios, and the null hypothesis that the cash spread is zero across all three YCC-sorted portfolios is not rejected.6.3. Asset pricing factor testWe next perform standard asset pricing tests to investigate whether the YCC premium can be explained by standard risk factors, as represented by the (Carhart, 1997) four-factor model and the (Fama and French, 2015) five-factor model.To test the standard risk-factor models, we perform time-series regressions of the excess returns of YCC-sorted portfolios on the (Carhart, 1997) four-factor model (FF3 factors and the momentum factor-MOM) in Panel A of Table C.11, and on the (Fama and French, 2015) five-factor model (FF3 factors, the profitability factor (RMW), and the investment factor (CMA)) in Panel B of Table C.11 in the Internet Appendix.As we show in Table C.11 of the Internet Appendix, the risk-adjusted returns (intercepts) of the YCC-sorted high-minus-low portfolio remain large and significant, ranging from 5.92% for the (Carhart, 1997) four-factor model in Panel A to 6.64% for the (Fama and French, 2015) five-factor model in Panel B with a t-statistic of 2.81, which is above the 1% statistical significance level. In addition, the alpha implied by these standard factor models remains comparable to the YCC spread (i.e., the return on the high-minus-low portfolio) in Table 3.Taken together, these results from the asset pricing tests in Table C.11 of the Internet Appendix suggest that the cross-sectional return spread across portfolios sorted on YCC cannot be explained by either the (Carhart, 1997) four-factor model or the (Fama and French, 2005) five-factor model. Hence, common risk factors cannot explain the higher returns associated with YCC.
